summary: Justin Brennan discusses his journey from real estate sales to multifamily syndication, detailing his strategy for entering new markets outside California. He emphasizes the importance of 6-month market prep, local relationship building, and understanding market-specific operational challenges (weather, soil conditions, seasonal rental cycles). While packed with general real estate philosophy and market commentary, the content offers limited actionable deal-analysis or underwriting specifics directly applicable to Brian's 15-50 unit value-add focus in GA/TN/AL.
takeaways: Enter new markets 6 months in advance to set up property management, construction crews, brokers, local banks, and legal/finance infrastructure before deal flow
Walk communities at 6-7pm on weekdays and weekends to observe tenant behavior and neighborhood quality before acquiring
Structure leases strategically around seasonal cycles (e.g., avoid November-February rentals in cold markets; target spring/fall turnovers)
Add washer/dryers in units as primary value-add—generates $200-400/month rent increase and extends tenant retention
Buy in 30-50 unit range first to learn logistics before scaling to 80-200+ units; mistakes on smaller deals cost less than on 50M deals
Maintain debt service coverage at 70-75% occupancy breakeven to weather 25-30% vacancy without investor principal loss
Kansas City example: buy at $56K-$83K per door, add value to reach $1,100-$1,200/door rents within years (significant value-add spread)

skill_candidates.change: Systematically vet new markets by analyzing job growth, median income, development plans, university systems, Fortune 500 presence, light rail/transit before committing capital
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin describes his Kansas City entry: researched job growth, 41 municipal colleges, 9 Fortune 500 companies, massive redevelopment; compared cost basis ($56K/$83K per door vs. San Diego's 3-cap) and rent growth potential (to $1,100-$1,200/door). Applied same rigor to Texas, Tucson, San Antonio markets.

skill_candidates.change: Underwrite small multifamily (2-4 unit) using revenue-based cap rate analysis rather than comps alone, matching commercial standards
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin mentions prepping financials for a 4-unit sale in El Cajon and underwrote it like a 10-20 unit deal using revenue, not just comps. Notes most brokers selling 2-4 units don't understand proper cap rate underwriting—'there's no way that's a cap rate.'

skill_candidates.change: Schedule lease rollovers intentionally across quarters and use 9-month, 18-month, or staggered lease terms to avoid seasonally-dead rental periods (Nov-Feb in cold climates)
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin describes Kansas City winter experience: November leases hard to rent, tenants want concessions; solution was staggering expirations so 'I want these coming due in spring into fall and that's it.' One unit rehabbed in Nov stayed vacant until spring.

title: BEST real estate Interview ever. "COVID won't be that bad" Feb 2020: 😳
